channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Elon Musk Becomes World’s First Trillionaire as SpaceX Stock Begins Trading
item.description: With SpaceX shares soaring on their first day of trading, the world’s richest person crossed another milestone — one with 13 digits.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac and Ben Casselman
item.media:description: Elon Musk, the chief executive of SpaceX, in 2022 at the company’s facility in Brownsville, Texas.

item.title: A Dangerous Limbo Leaves Iran, and the World, Between Peace and War
item.description: Since announcing a nominal cease-fire two months ago, Iran, Israel and the U.S. have remained locked in low-intensity violence that has become a new normal.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Tehran on Monday, a billboard featuring the Iran theocracy’s first two supreme leaders loomed over passers-by.

item.title: The World Is Draining Oil Reserves, Raising Pressure for a Peace Deal
item.description: The amount of oil and fuel stored by businesses and governments has fallen sharply since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lazaro Gamio
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Despite Talk of an Iran Peace Deal, Lebanon’s War Grinds On
item.description: Israeli strikes on Friday left Lebanon out of sync with a cautious optimism taking hold elsewhere in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Residents survey damage following an Israeli airstrike in the southern port city of Tyre on Friday.

item.title: FISA, a Key U.S. Spying Power, Is Expiring. What Does That Mean for Foreign Surveillance?
item.description: Top officials and some lawmakers say letting a powerful spying authority expire on Saturday will leave the United States dangerously blind. But surveillance can still continue.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson said that not extending the law, known as Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, risked “a serious calamity on our shores.”

item.title: David Hockney, Who Restored the Human Form to Art, Dies at 88
item.description: His colorful figurative paintings were both conservative and iconoclastic, defying the dominant abstract schools of the mid-20th century.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter

item.title: Framing David Hockney’s Greatest Art
item.description: Whether in Los Angeles, in his native England or traveling the world, the artist always reinvented the world he saw, with psychological insight.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter

item.title: David Hockney’s Sense of Style Never Wavered
item.description: The painter David Hockney’s clothes roared with mirth and maximalism.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Nathanael Turner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Color, so integral to David Hockney’s paintings, was part of his outfits as well.

item.title: Inside Cesar Chavez’s Labor Union, Women Faced a Culture of Sexual Abuse
item.description: Many of the women who helped lead the fight for Latino civil rights say they were quietly waging their own battles with harassment, misogyny and sexual assault inside the powerful United Farm Workers union.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Hurtes and Manny Fernandez
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Plan Is Said to Pull a Third of Fighter Jets It Provides NATO for Europe
item.description: The plan, outlined by officials and in a written document, provides rare clarity about the extent to which the Trump administration intends to reduce its commitment to NATO.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: An American F-16 jet at Andravida air base, Greece, last year.

item.title: After Senate Loss, Cornyn Predicts ‘Miserable’ Final Two Years for Trump
item.description: In his first extensive interview since his defeat by a Trump-backed challenger, the Texas Republican said the Senate was in for a “bumpy ride” as he and others flex new political freedom.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: “There’s never going to be good enough for him, other than 100 percent, you know, slavish adherence to whatever he wants,” Senator John Cornyn said of President Trump. “But obviously that’s not what the senator’s role is supposed to be.”

item.title: Can Democrats Overcome G.O.P. Gerrymandering?
item.description: Our chief political analyst, Nate Cohn, who writes The Tilt newsletter, looks at the Republicans’ advantage in the House of Representatives after partisan redistricting. To win the House, how much of the popular vote would Democrats need to win?
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn, Laura Bult, June Kim, Edward Vega and Pierre Kattar

item.title: Rick Jackson, Georgia Governor Candidate, Is Also a Film Producer Battling the IRS
item.description: Rick Jackson, a billionaire, wants to be Georgia’s next governor. But the I.R.S. has questioned how his low-budget movies turned into tax windfalls.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim
item.media:credit: Dustin Chambers for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rick Jackson has sought $90 million in tax deductions from four money-losing films.

item.title: AIPAC Wants Democrats to Back Israel. Instead, They’re Turning on AIPAC.
item.description: Once the guardian of the bipartisan pro-Israel consensus, it is now a polarizing force in the party.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Mahler

item.title: Suspect in Fatal Shooting Is Dead After a Standoff in Midland, Texas, Officials Say
item.description: The man was confirmed to be dead after he had barricaded himself in a building in Midland, following an earlier shooting on Friday that killed one person and injured at least 11 others.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman
item.media:description: In an image provided by local news, law enforcement officers responded to reports of an active shooter in Midland, Texas.

item.title: She Was Dying of Cancer. How Should She Live?
item.description: Thanks to new treatments, some patients with Stage 4 cancer are living longer — leaving them in limbo for years.
item.dc:creator: Nina Agrawal
item.media:credit: Ben Brewer for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s D.C. Renovations, HGTV Style
item.description: President Trump brings a particular brand of reality TV-style renovations to the nation’s capital.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: After generations of slow and often staid improvements, the Trump era has already added a splashy flair to today’s Washington, including an Ultimate Fighting Championship octagon cage rising on the South Lawn, ready to host a fight to celebrate the nation’s 250th birthday.

item.title: Fact-Check: Trump’s Claims About His Arch, Reflecting Pool Repairs and Other Renovation Projects
item.description: President Trump has made false or exaggerated claims of a Civil War-era push for a triumphal arch, hundreds of millions spent on repairs on the Reflecting Pool, and an absence of working fountains.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu and Jamie Leventhal

item.title: Knicks in Five? Please Don’t Tempt the Jinx Gods.
item.description: Some Knicks fans have predicted series sweeps. Others, conditioned by decades of failure, pay more heed to jinxes and superstitious rituals.
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Knicks’ championship run has revealed a fundamental difference among some fans, with some unafraid of making boastful predictions and others recoiling at the jinx factor of doing so.

item.title: Brawls, Biting and Bliss in a City Craving a Knicks Championship
item.description: Decades of bottled-up hopes and frustrations have created a frenzy around the New York Knicks that has brought out the worst in some fans.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer and Maia Coleman
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers stood guard as Game 4 of the N.B.A. finals drew a massive number of fans to Midtown Manhattan.

item.title: A Guide to Chicago’s South Side, Home of the New Obama Center
item.description: The Obama Presidential Center, opening on June 19, is poised to attract visitors to an often-overlooked area. Here’s what you’ll find in Hyde Park and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac and Akilah Townsend
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:description: The $850-million Obama Presidential Center will introduce visitors to an area they have largely overlooked in favor of downtown attractions. Above, the sculpture of the Obamas outside the center was created by StudioEIS, a Brooklyn-based design studio.

item.title: SpaceX’s I.P.O. Will Help Fulfill Elon Musk’s Political Vision of America
item.description: The SpaceX I.P.O. will enable its founder to build — and scale — his peculiar vision of society.
item.dc:creator: Amy Gamerman and Tam Stockton

item.title: Some Leftists Are Boycotting This Film. Everyone Should Watch It.
item.description: The inanity of the leftists who’d censor such a film shouldn’t distract us from the right-wing nightmare it reveals.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times

item.title: A Trillionaire?
item.description: SpaceX is expected to make Elon Musk even richer when its stock begins trading today.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Late Night Prepares for Trump’s U.F.C. Birthday Fight at the White House
item.description: Jimmy Fallon called the president “the only 80-year-old yelling, ‘Get on my lawn!’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Ohio City Worker Union Complains That Goats Are Eating Its Lunch
item.description: A union in Columbus made a formal complaint after the city farmed out brush-clearing duties at a wastewater plant. The competitors? Goats.
item.dc:creator: Sejal Govindarao
item.category: American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees
